system_context.system_name: Large, real-time, embedded process control system [cite: 16]
system_context.business_goals: Ultra-high availability, performance, and the ability to extract a functionally useful subset from the system[cite: 59].

architectures.name: Distributed Ada Real-Time Control System
architectures.description: A million-line Ada-based embedded system designed for large-scale process control. It is built to be distributed across multiple nodes, ensuring high reliability and the ability to extract functional subsets for specific deployment scenarios.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Ultra-high availability, Deterministic Performance, Modularity, Fault Tolerance, Distributed Execution

key_components.responsibilities: Executes real-time control logic and handles direct interfacing with embedded hardware processes.
Manages distributed message passing and ensures synchronization across the distributed Ada environment.
Manages operator interactions and data visualization under strict human interface constraints.
Monitors system health, manages redundancy, and ensures ultra-high availability through error recovery.

architectures.data_flow: Real-time process data flows from sensors to Distributed Process Controllers; state updates are replicated via the Communication Middleware to ensure fault-tolerance, while summarized telemetry is pushed to the Human Interface Subsystem.
architectures.notes: The architecture is highly optimized for reliability and can accommodate functional subsetting for different deployments. However, it cannot easily accommodate non-deterministic workloads or integration with non-Ada legacy systems without significant bridging overhead.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenario_classification.rationale: The architecture was explicitly designed for modularity and the extraction of functional subsets to support distributed deployment scenarios as stated in the business goals and description.

The system is strictly Ada-based and optimized for deterministic performance; integrating non-Ada legacy systems is noted as a significant challenge requiring bridging overhead that violates current constraints.
Fault tolerance and ultra-high availability are core architectural characteristics, managed by a dedicated Health Manager component designed for error recovery.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Minimal configuration changes to define the functional subset and node mapping; no structural code changes required.

Implementation of Ada-to-External language bindings (FFI), development of a synchronization bridge in the middleware to handle non-deterministic timing from legacy components, and modification of data types for cross-environment compatibility.
issues_identified: Risk of breaking deterministic performance deadlines due to bridging overhead
Memory management and safety concerns at the Ada/Legacy boundary
Complexity in maintaining fault-tolerance across heterogeneous environments
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Isolate the legacy system on a separate hardware node and communicate via a strictly defined network protocol through a dedicated gateway component to preserve the core system's determinism.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required; the Health Manager (C-04) and Communication Middleware (C-02) are designed to handle state replication and failover automatically.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: C-02 is the most critical hotspot as it is the only component participating in all three scenarios. Its involvement in S-02 is particularly high-risk, as it acts as a synchronization bridge for legacy timing. Failure to address the architectural complexity here threatens the system's deterministic performance deadlines and fault-tolerance, directly impacting the end-user experience and the maintainer's ability to debug cross-environment issues.

C-01 is a primary site for the widespread changes required in S-02, specifically regarding Ada-to-External language bindings. While it is hit by fewer scenarios than C-02, the technical complexity of memory management and safety at the language boundary represents a significant risk for developers. If not addressed, this component will become a source of memory leaks and safety violations that are difficult for maintainers to trace.

architectural_strengths: High Availability and Fault Tolerance: The architecture demonstrates robust automatic state replication and failover capabilities through the Health Manager (C-04) and Communication Middleware (C-02), as evidenced by the low-effort, low-risk rating for S-03.

Functional Flexibility: The system supports the strategic business goal of extracting functional subsets with minimal effort, requiring only configuration changes rather than structural code modifications (S-01).

Effective Component Decoupling for Internal Logic: The separation of concerns between health management and communication allows the system to handle failure scenarios without manual intervention or architectural rework.
architectural_weaknesses: Fragile External Interoperability: The architecture struggles with legacy integration (S-02), requiring widespread changes and posing a high risk to deterministic performance deadlines and memory safety at the Ada/External boundary.

Critical Component Overloading (C-02): The Communication Middleware is a major hotspot involved in every evaluated scenario. Its role as a synchronization bridge for legacy timing (S-02) creates a significant risk of architectural 'bottlenecking' and cross-environment instability.

Inadequate Abstraction for Heterogeneous Environments: The current design requires complex FFI and manual data type modifications for cross-environment compatibility, indicating a lack of a standardized isolation layer for non-native components.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture is highly modifiable regarding internal functional scaling and availability configurations, but it exhibits significant rigidity and high-risk complexity when evolving to support heterogeneous or legacy integrations.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement a Gateway Pattern for Legacy Integration
recommendations.rationale: Isolating legacy components (S-02) on separate hardware nodes using a dedicated gateway will preserve core system determinism and eliminate memory safety risks at the Ada boundary, addressing the highest-ranked stakeholder concern.

recommendations.recommendation: Refactor C-02 Middleware to Decouple Synchronization Logic
recommendations.rationale: Since C-02 is a hotspot for all scenarios, separating its core communication functions from the complex synchronization bridging required for legacy timing will reduce the risk of breaking performance deadlines across the entire system.

Developing a formal automated layer for cross-environment data type compatibility will reduce the 'Widespread' change extent identified in S-02 and improve maintainability for developers and maintainers.

To ensure the long-term viability of the 'subset extraction' business goal, the current configuration-based approach (S-01) should be formalized into a validated schema to prevent misconfiguration during node mapping.
